one_line_takeaway: A qualitative survey (not the detection tool its title suggests) that taxonomizes MCP server threats into agent-hijacking, traditional web, and supply-chain categories and surveys server-side-scanning and interaction-monitoring defenses, arguing MCP shifts the attack surface from code execution to semantic interpretation of tool metadata.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is conceptual, not empirical: MCP's core design decision to treat natural-language tool metadata as authoritative, trusted input for LLM decision-making, with no mechanism to verify its provenance, integrity, or semantic safety, shifts the attack surface from classical code execution to semantic interpretation. Consequently, MCP supply-chain security 'has effectively become a battleground for adversarial prompt engineering,' and defenses must be tailored to this new threat model rather than reusing traditional signature/hash/sandbox controls.
core_contribution: Despite a title that implies a novel detection tool ('MCPGuard: Automatically Detecting Vulnerabilities in MCP Servers'), the paper is actually a systematization-of-knowledge / survey of the security landscape of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Its own contribution is a taxonomy that organizes MCP security into (1) a threat side with three principal categories and (2) a defense side with two paradigms. The three threat categories are: agent-hijacking risks arising from MCP protocol design deficiencies (tool poisoning, rug pulls, tool shadowing, indirect prompt injection); traditional/code-level web vulnerabilities in MCP servers (command injection, path traversal, arbitrary file read, SSRF, XSS, insecure deserialization, SQL injection, broken auth); and supply-chain security (untrusted marketplaces, tool squatting, MCP Preference Manipulation Attacks). The two defense paradigms are proactive server-side scanning and runtime interaction monitoring. The paper's synthesizing thesis is that MCP security represents a paradigm shift in which the attack surface moves 'upstream' from code execution to the semantic interpretation of natural-language tool metadata, which cannot be validated by classical signatures/hashes/sandboxing.
threat_model: Adversary controls or spoofs an MCP server (or a tool within one) and exploits the fact that MCP clients inject tool descriptions, parameter schemas, and tool outputs directly into the LLM's reasoning context with no isolation between different tools' sessions and no provenance/integrity verification of that natural-language metadata. Attack vectors surveyed: tool poisoning (malicious instructions embedded in a tool description field, e.g., the whatsapp-mcp get_fact_of_the_day tool exfiltrating chat history, disclosed by Invariant Labs); tool shadowing (registering a tool with a legitimate tool's name plus 'global rules' in its description, exploiting absence of namespace isolation); rug pulls (a trusted server later silently backdoored via remote update, since MCP lacks version signing/change auditing); indirect prompt injection via untrusted tool outputs; supply-chain / MPMA preference-manipulation attacks that optimize tool names/descriptions to be preferentially selected by agents; and classic web exploits when servers are deployed remotely over HTTP with unsanitized LLM-supplied parameters passed to eval/os.system/exec or SQL. A concrete real-world anchor is CVE-2025-49596, an RCE in Anthropic's MCP Inspector.
limitations: (1) No original technical contribution, system, or evaluation: contrary to the title, no vulnerability-detection tool is built or measured; the 'automatic detection' referenced in the conclusion is an existing third-party tool (AI-Infra-Guard/AIG). (2) No quantitative results, datasets, baselines, precision/recall, or coverage numbers whatsoever, so no claims can be reproduced or compared. (3) Purely descriptive survey with no systematic methodology stated (no inclusion criteria, search protocol, or paper-selection process), and a fairly small reference base (18 references). (4) Naming collision risk: the paper's title 'MCPGuard' is distinct from the surveyed 'MCP-Guard' [11] and 'MCP Guardian' [17], which can confuse attribution. (5) The taxonomy overlaps categories (indirect prompt injection appears under both agent-hijacking and supply-chain framing), and defenses are not mapped rigorously to the threats they mitigate.
